Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## NUMERIC ENCODING OPTIONS WITH AUTOMUNGE

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Mainstream practice in machine learning with tabular data may take for granted that any feature engineering beyond scaling for numeric sets is superfluous in context of deep neural networks. This paper will offer arguments for potential benefits of extended encodings of numeric streams in deep learning by way of a survey of options for numeric transformations as available in the Automunge open source python library platform for tabular data pipelines, where transformations may be applied to distinct columns in “family tree” sets with generations and branches of derivations. Automunge transformation options include normalization, binning, noise injection, derivatives, and more. The aggregation of these methods into family tree sets of transformations are demonstrated for use to present numeric features to machine learning in multiple configurations of varying information content, as may be applied to encode numeric sets of unknown interpretation. Experiments demonstrate the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning, as may materially benefit model performance in applications with underserved training data.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Of the various modalities of machine learning application (e.g. images, language, audio, etc.) tabular data, aka structured data, as may comprise tables of feature set columns and collected sample rows, in my experience does not command as much attention from the research community, for which I speculate may be partly attributed to the general non-uniformity across manifestations precluding the conventions of most other modalities for representative benchmarks and availability of pre-trained architectures as could be adapted with fine-tuning to practical applications. That is not to say that tabular data lacks points of uniformity across data sets, for at its core the various feature sets can at a high level be grouped into just two primary types: numeric and categoric. It was the focus of a recent paper by this author (Author, 2020) to explore methods of preparing categoric sets for machine learning as are available in the Automunge open source python library platform for tabular data pipelines. This paper will give similar treatment for methods to prepare numeric feature sets for machine learning.

Of course it would be an to characterize “numeric feature sets” as a descriptor alone to represent the wide amount of diversity as may be found between different such instances. Numeric could be referring to integers, floats, or combinations thereof. The set of entries could be bounded, the potential range of entries could be bounded on the left, right, or both sides, the distribution of values could be thin or fat tailed, single or multi-modal. The order of samples could be independent or sequential. In some cases the values could themselves be an encoded representation of a categoric feature.

Beyond the potential diversity found within our numeric features, another source of diversity could be considered based on relationships between multiple feature sets. For example one feature could be independent of the others, could contain full or partial redundancy with one or more other variables by correlation, or in the case of sequential data there could even be causal relationships between variables across time steps.

The primary focus of transformations to be discussed in this paper will not take into account variable interdependencies, and will instead operate under the assumption that the training operation of a downstream learning algorithm may be more suitable for the efficient interpretation of such interdependencies, as the convention for Automunge is that data transformations (and in some cases

sets of transformations) are to be directed for application to a distinct feature set as input. In many cases the basis for these transformations will be properties derived from the received feature in a designated “train” set (as may be passed to the automunge(.) function) for subsequent application on a consistent basis to a designated “test” set (as may be passed to the postmunge(.) function).

## 2 NORMALIZATIONS

A common practice for preprocessing numeric feature sets for the application of neural networks is to apply a normalization operation in which received values are centered and scaled based on properties of the data. By conversion to comparable scale between features, backpropagation may have an easier time navigating the fitness landscape rather than overweighting to higher magnitude inputs (Ng, 2011). Table 1 surveys a few normalization operations as available in Automunge.

Table 1: Normalizations

|**Type**|**ID**|**Formula**|**Scaling**|
|---|---|---|---|
|z-score|‘nmbr’|(_xi −µ_)_/σ_|scaled to sigma 1 and mu 0|
|min-max|‘mnmx’|(_xi −min_)_/_(_max −min_)|scaled to unit interval|
|mean|‘mean’|(_xi −mean_)_/_(_max −min_)|scaled and centered to mean|
|MAD|‘MAD3’|(_xi −max_)_/_(_MAD_)|scaled by median absolute deviation|
|lognorm|‘lgnm’|ln(_xi_)_!_(_xi −µ_)_/σ_|log-normal scaled to Gaussian|

Upon inspection a few points of differentiation become evident. The choice of denominator can be material to the result, for while both (max - min) and standard deviation can have the result of shrinking or enlarging the values to fall within a more uniform range, the (max - min) variety has more of a known returned range for the output that is independent of the feature set distribution properties, thus allowing us to ensure all of the min-max returned values are non-negative for instance, as may be a pre-requisite for some kinds of algorithms. Of course this known range of output relies on the assumption that the range of values in subsequent test sets will correspond to the train set properties that serve as a basis - to allow a user to prevent these type of outliers from interfering with downstream applications, Automunge allows a user to pass parameters to the transformation functions, such as to activate floors or caps on the returned range.

An easy to overlook outcome of the shifting and/or centering of the returned range by way of the subtraction operation in the numerator is a loss of the original zero point, as for example with z-score normalization the returned zero point is shifted to coincide with the original mean. It is the opinion of this author that such re-centering of the data may not always be a trivial trade-off. Consider the special properties of the number 0 in mathematics, including multiplicative properties at/above/below. By shifting the original zero point we are presenting a (small) obstacle to the training operation in order to relearn this point. Perhaps more importantly, further trade-offs include the interpretability of the returned data.

Automunge thus offers a novel form of normalization, available in our library as ‘retn’ (standing for “retain”), that bases the formula applied to scale data on the range of values found within the train set, with the result of scaling the data within a similar range as some of those demonstrated above while also retaining the zero point and thus the +/- sign of all received data.

Table 2: Retain Normalization (‘retn’)

|__0|_≥_0|_xi/_(_max −min_)|_min/_(_max −min_)|_max/_(_max −min_)|
|_>_0|_>_0|(_xi −min_)_/_(_max −min_)|0|1|
|_<_0|_<_0|(_xi −max_)_/_(_max −min_)|-1|0|

## 3 TRANSFORMATIONS

In many cases the application of a normalization procedure may be preceded by one or more types of data transformations applied to the received numeric set. Examples of data transformations could include basic mathematic operators like + - * /, log transforms, raising to a power, absolute values, etc. In some cases the transformations may also be tailored to the properties of the train set, for example with a Box-Cox power law transformation (Box & Cox, 1964).

In the Automunge library, the order of such sets of transformations, as may be applied to a distinct source column, and in some cases which may include generations and branches of derivations, are specified by way of transformation category entries to a set of “family tree” primitives [Table 3] (Author, 2020) for a root transformation category, and where a transformation category entry may be associated with one or more transformation functions intended for application to corresponding train and/or test set feature columns, potentially including custom transformation functions which may be defined with minimal requirements of simple data structures. Such root categories may be pre-defined in the Automunge library of transformations or may be custom configured by a user in entries to a “transformdict” data structure.

The convention for transformation functions in the Automunge library is that any kind of function accepts any kind of data, and in cases where an invalid entry is returned, for example when dividing by zero or taking a square root of a negative number, such entry may serve as a target for missing data infill, with infill methods that may be applied to a column from a library of infill options - including “ML infill” in which random forest models (Breiman, 2001) are used to predict infill based on properties of the train set. To facilitate the application of infill, transformation categories used as root categories are specified with a classification for the types of data that will be considered valid input, as for example may be non-negative numeric, non-zero numeric, integer numeric, etc. These “NArowtype” classifications are populated in the same “processdict” data structure used to assign transformation functions to a transformation category.

## 4 BINS AND GRAININGS

In most cases the transformations considered in the preceding section maintained full information retention of the received data, such that with returned sets the form of the input data can be recovered with an inversion operation (as is available in the Automunge library). For binning transformations, there may instead be a type of coarse graining of the feature set to aggregate buckets of entries into a categoric representation. Automunge offers a wide range of options for numeric binning [Table 4]. Bins may be aggregated to either supplement or replace received numeric sets.

For each binning operation, transformation category options are available to return the categoric encoding as a one-hot encoding, ordinal integer encoding, or binary encoding in which distinct categories may be represented by multiple simultaneous activations. This is partly motivated by different conventions of various libraries for accepting input to an entity embedding layer (Guo & Berkhahn, 2016) as may be applied to the returned categoric encoding in a downstream training operation.

## 5 NOISE INJECTION

For most cases in the Automunge library, transformations applied to a train set feature set are applied to the corresponding test set feature set using the same basis, such that if the same data is received for both train and test sets, the same form will be returned (a useful point for validations). The noise injection options are a little different in that such injections may be intended just for the train data but not the corresponding test data.

et al., 2006). Other potential uses of noise injections could be to perturb the model training, facilitating diversity between models as may be beneficial in the aggregation of ensembles (Dietterich, 2000) or as a source of training data augmentation (Perez & Wang, 2017). The experiments detailed below will suggest a material model performance benefit from data augmentation by noise injection in cases of underserved training data, which we believe is a novel innovation for tabular learning.

The options available for noise injection are generally derived by way of aggregations of transformation category entries to family tree primitives, as noise injection may be applied downstream to a normalization or categoric encoding. (The convention for transformation functions is that they receive input of a single target column, so transformations performed downstream of a categoric encoding should be fed an ordinal input.) The library includes distinct noise injection family tree aggregations tailored to operation of several different types of received normalizations, as may rely on a known range or scale of input, or applied preceding different types of categoric encodings.

Numeric noise injections [Table 5, Fig 1] are derived from a Gaussian source with configurable parameters. For noise intended to sets with a fixed range of values such as DPmm, although the

noise source as implemented is Gaussian, the application is capped from extreme outliers at half of range (e.g. +/- 0.5) and based on whether an input entry is above or below the midpoint, positive or negative noise respectively is scaled to ensure maintained original range in returned data based on values of input entry. Parameters are also accepted to indicate what ratio of input will receive injection. Similar options are available for Laplace distribution noise profiles.

To clarify how family tree primitives come into play, for the ‘DPmm’ root category in Fig 1, when ‘DPmm’ is applied as a root category to a source column with header ‘column’, the upstream primitive entries are inspected, which include a parents entry of the ‘DPm2’ transformation category and a cousins entry of the ‘NArw’ transformation category. For the ‘NArw’ upstream entry to the cousins primitive, the ‘NArw’ transformation category is associated with a NArw transformation function (a function aggregating boolean activations indicating presence of infill) per the processdict entry associated with the ‘NArw’ root category, which transformation function returns the column ‘column ~~N~~ Arw’ with the suffix appender ‘ ~~N~~ Arw’ logging the transformation function applied. Because cousins is a primitive without offspring no further generations are inspected in the ‘NArw’ family tree downstream primitives. For the upstream parents primitive entry ‘DPm2’, the ‘DPm2’ processdict entry has a transformation function of mnmx (min-max scaling), which is applied and logged by the ‘ ~~m~~ nmx’ suffix appender, and since parents is a primitive with offspring the downstream primitives in the ‘DPm2’ family tree are inspected where a coworkers entry of ‘DPmm’ transformation category is found which is associated with a DPmm transformation function (noise injection corresponding to range 0-1) based on the ‘DPmm’ root category processdict entry. Because coworkers is a replacement primitive the column configuration with header ‘column ~~m~~ nmx’ is not retained for the returned set. The returned column originating from the DPmm transformation function has header logging the two applied transformation functions as ‘column ~~m~~ nmx ~~D~~ Pmm’. Please note that column retention, as may be impacted by the application of downstream replacement primitive entries, is signaled in these diagrams by the color coding between orange and blue, and applied transformation functions are shaded as green. Transformation category entries of primitives that are not inspected are left without shading. Also please note that the processdict entries here are an abstraction for the set of corresponding transformation functions that may be directed to train and/or test set feature sets.

The application of noise to categoric encodings [Table 6, Fig 2] is a little simpler, where a given ratio of entries in a categoric feature set are flipped to one of the other activations, between which have a uniform probability of replacement (including possibility of original entry retention). For the ‘DP10’ root category example shown in Fig 2, this is achieved by first applying an ordinal encoding by ord3 transformation function associated with a ‘DPo6’ transformation category entry to the parents upstream primitive of the ‘DP10’ root category, then a noise injection by the DPod transformation function associated with a ‘DPo3’ transformation category to the downstream children primitive associated with the ‘DPo6’ root category (applied since parents is a primitive with offspring), followed by a binary encoding by the 1010 transformation function associated with the ‘1010’ transformation category entry to the downstream coworkers primitive associated with the ‘DPo3’ root category (applied since children is a primitive with offspring). Here the intermediate stages of derivations associated with columns ‘column ~~o~~ rd3’ and ‘column ~~o~~ rd3 ~~D~~ Pod’ are not retained in the returned set since children and coworkers are replacement primitives.

## 6 SEQUENTIAL DATA

For numeric features in which the order of samples carry some e.g. for time series data, some additional options are available to extract structure from relationships between time steps (these methods may benefit from a convention that time deltas between measurements are at or nearly constant). The theory is that for sequential machine learning applications, as may make use of recurrence, convolution, or attention mechanisms, there may be benefit to supplementing feature streams with properties carried forward from prior time steps, where this type of operation may be particularly beneficial when there is some known cyclic property inherent in the application, e.g. for scheduled trading hours or quarterly reports.

Included in the Automunge library are sequential transforms to supplement sequential streams with proxies for derivatives by returning deltas between an entry and some desired time step prior by way of the ‘dxdt’ family of transforms [Fig 3]. Such application may be applied once as a proxy for velocity, and may also be run multiple times upon that output as proxies for higher order derivatives. A variant on this operation may, instead of taking point-wise deltas, return deltas between averages of sets of points, which has the effect of smoothing or de-noising the data. The outputs of these operations may each be normalized such as with the ‘retn’ normalization for sign retention.

Figure 3: ‘dxdt’ family trees

## 7 INTEGER SETS

Integer feature sets of unknown origin may present a particular challenge for automated encodings, as these may be associated with a diverse set of interpretations, and may originate from continuous variables, counters, discrete relational variables (e.g. small/medium/large), or possibly even an ordinal categoric encoding (Stevens et al., 2020). The Automunge philosophy for these kind of ambiguities is to simply redundantly encode in a manner suitable for each [Table 7, Fig 4], defering to a training operation for determining relevancy.

## 8 EXPERIMENTS

Some experiments were run to evaluate impact of various normalizations and transformations. The Higgs data set (Baldi et al., 2014) was selected based on scale and predominantly numeric feature types. The Higgs application is tabular data for binary classification sourced from high energy physics domain to evaluate subatomic particle interactions to distinguish between background noise and traces of Higgs boson interactions. The origin paper noted some details of architectures (5 layers with 300 nodes in each) that served as basis for the experiments, and we used a 0.6M size validation set out of the 8.8M samples. A departure was made from the origin paper in use of a phased learning rate with the fastai (Howard & Gugger, 2020) “fit ~~o~~ ne ~~c~~ ycle” learner for tabular data, partly for convenience along with time and resource constraints. Since the interest was primarily to evaluate relative performance between different preprocessing methods, no significant attempt was made to train models to maximum performance or to venture beyond double descent, instead the primary tuning aspect was selecting an epoch depth beyond which additional did not have improved validation metrics. The experiments were repeated with different size subsets of the training data, including full data set, 5% samples, and 0.25% samples to represent scenarios with underserved training data. Learning rates deferred to the fastai default finder, and the evaluation metric was selected as area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC AUC) to be consistent with the origin demonstration.

The experiments applied Automunge to preprocess the feature sets, in each case applying uniform transformation types between features although with basis fit to properties of each respective column, and with missing data infill by way of the Automunge adjacent cell infill option. The types of transformations were selected to demonstrate impact of a few representative variants noted in this writeup, and included a base scenario with no feature scaling applied (just raw numeric data), along with a scenario for z-score normalization, retain normalization, retain normalization supplemented by standard deviation bins, two scenarios of retain normalization with noise injection (with noise injected to all entries or to 3% of entries in each feature), and finally a data augmentation with retain normalized data supplemented by doubling the number of samples to include retain with partial

The AUC metric shown compares to the origin paper’s reported 88%, which was based on a reported 200-1,000 epochs compared to this experiment’s 3-42 so there is some dissimilarity in results, which can also partly be attributed to the phased learning rate schedule applied by fastai. The AUC metrics shown are averages of repetition counts detailed in Appendix G.

The scenarios for raw data, z-score, and retain normalization achieved similar results. Part of the impact of supplementing the normalized data with standard deviation bins was a small increase of training time. As expected the noise injection had a dampening effect on the model accuracy, although the partial injection had only a small penalty in comparison to the retain normalization without injection. Data augmentation improved the results, sufficiently to draw the conclusion of material benefit for scenarios with underserved training data. We believe the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning is a significant finding.

## 9 DISCUSSION

first by scale of data available for training. When approaching big data scale input with infinite computational resources there may be less of a case to be made for much beyond basic normalized input. The nuance comes into play when we are targeting applications with real world constraints. Although deep over-parameterized models may still be applied to target applications with underrepresented data (Olson et al., 2018), such methods carry overheads. It is one of the premises of

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

the Automunge library that supplementing our data streams with redundant features in multiple configurations may lower the bar to efficient extraction of inter-variable relationships.

My experience is that the machine learning community has by large become somewhat dismissive of any kind of feature engineering, I expect partly owed to such works as Deep Learning (Goodfellow et al., 2016) which offers that deep learning has supplanted the need for such effort. I would offer in retort that there are different kinds of feature engineering to consider. Those methods as may apply explorations and optimizations between inter-variable relationships I agree are largely redundant of what may be efficiently derived through backpropagation and this is part of the reason why Automunge hasn’t ventured into this territory. But the types of data manipulations as may be used to supplement numeric features with multiple configurations are computationally efficient, and need not require sophisticated optimizations to apply. The primary cost of such supplements are the memory overheads as the data set size is expanded.

Further, variations on feature composition, such as by variations in information content and variations on injected noise, may serve as a useful source of model perturbations in ensemble assemblies. Noise injection may be of benefit for anonymizing sensitive data for purposes of differential privacy, and may also serve as a source of training data augmentation, similar to how for convolutional networks training data images may be duplicated with artificial variations (Perez & Wang, 2017).

I’ll close by noting an interesting paper I saw at NeurIPS last year, “SGD on Neural Networks Learn Functions of Increasing Complexity” by Nakkiran et al. (2019), in which the authors found that SGD behaves as a linear model in early epochs, and importantly that characteristics of such linear models are retained even in the later stages of training. Put differently, any steps that may be made to ensure that our models may efficiently extract properties even in early stages, before deep learning can do its magic, are not immaterial to the final performance.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a comprehensive overview of numeric encoding options in the Automunge library, with a focus on practical transformations like normalization, binning, noise injection, and family-tree derivations. It makes a plausible case for the utility of redundant and multi-view encoding, particularly for low-data regimes, and introduces a novel noise injection framework inspired by data augmentation and differential privacy. The discussion connects well to prior work on feature engineering and tabular deep learning, and the experiments on the Higgs dataset provide initial empirical grounding.

Overview issues:

- Lack of comparison with standard preprocessing baselines: The paper evaluates several Automunge-specific transformations but does not compare against standard, widely adopted preprocessing baselines such as quantile transforms, robust scaling, or power transformations (e.g., Box-Cox, Yeo-Johnson), which are standard in tabular ML workflows. Table 8 compares raw data, z-score, and retain normalization, but omits established alternatives that could better contextualize the claimed benefits. Without such comparisons, it is unclear whether the observed effects are due to the novelty of Automunge's methods or simply the act of applying any reasonable preprocessing. This omission undermines the paper's contribution claim, especially given the abstract's suggestion of broad relevance beyond a single library.

- Insufficient ablation of noise injection and augmentation effects: The paper claims a 'material model performance benefit' from data augmentation via noise injection, particularly in low-data regimes. However, the ablation is incomplete: the 'Retain w/ Augment' condition doubles the dataset size by adding noisy copies, but does not control for the effect of sample duplication alone. It is unclear whether the improvement comes from noise injection, increased sample count, or their combination. A proper ablation would compare duplication without noise, noise without duplication, and both. Furthermore, the noise injection is applied at 3% or 100% of entries, but no analysis justifies these rates or explores their sensitivity. This weakens the claim of a 'generalized solution' to augmentation.

- Overstated novelty of noise injection for tabular data: The paper repeatedly claims novelty for noise injection as a data augmentation technique in tabular learning. However, noise injection—particularly Gaussian and Laplace—is a well-known regularization and privacy technique, and its use in tabular models (e.g., in dropout-like schemes or differential privacy) is established. The 'DPnb', 'DPmm' variants are minor adaptations of known methods, not conceptual innovations. While the integration into a preprocessing pipeline may be novel, the core idea is not. The literature context cites Dwork et al. (2006) and Perez & Wang (2017), but fails to position the work accurately within this prior art, leading to an inflated novelty claim.

- Lack of reproducibility and transparency around Automunge: The paper depends heavily on the Automunge library, yet provides no public code link, no version information, and no details on how transformations are implemented or parameterized (e.g., how 'retain normalization' handles edge cases). The family tree system is complex, but the paper offers no open-sourced configuration or script to reproduce the Higgs experiments. This violates basic reproducibility standards in ML research. Without access to the code or a detailed schema of transformation pipelines, the experiments cannot be verified or extended by others, limiting the paper's scientific value.

- Unclear generalization and automated selection claims: The paper implies that Automunge's encoding options can be applied automatically to 'numeric sets of unknown interpretation' (Section 1) and that the framework generalizes well. However, no automated selection mechanism is described or evaluated. The experiments apply uniform transformations across all features, which is unrealistic for real-world tabular data where features have heterogeneous types and scales. The absence of a strategy for choosing or combining encodings—beyond manual configuration—undermines the claim of a practical, general-purpose solution. A top-tier venue would expect either an automated selector or a principled study of encoding compatibility.

- Inadequate evaluation across diverse datasets: All experiments are conducted on a single dataset—the Higgs dataset—despite the paper's broad claims about applicability to 'underserved training data' and 'unknown interpretation' features. The Higgs dataset is large, predominantly numeric, and high-dimensional, which limits the generalizability of the findings. The paper should include at least one additional benchmark with mixed numeric-categorical features, real-world sparsity, or different domain characteristics (e.g., healthcare, finance). Without this, the results may be dataset-specific and fail to support the paper's broader ambitions.

- No ablation of sample duplication vs. noise in augmentation: The paper claims a material benefit from data augmentation via noise injection, particularly in low-data regimes, but fails to disentangle the effect of noise from that of sample duplication. The 'Retain w/ Augment' condition doubles the dataset by adding noisy copies, yet there is no control condition with duplicated samples without noise. As a result, it is unclear whether the observed gains come from increased sample size, the injected noise, or their interaction. This undermines the claim of a 'generalized solution' to augmentation. To resolve this, the authors should add a new experiment comparing three variants: (1) duplication without noise, (2) noise injection without duplication, and (3) both, on the 0.25% and 5% Higgs subsets. Only then can the contribution of noise be isolated.

- Missing comparison to standard preprocessing baselines: The evaluation compares raw data, z-score, and retain normalization, but omits standard preprocessing methods like quantile transforms, robust scaling, or Yeo-Johnson transformations, which are widely used in tabular ML. Without these, it is impossible to assess whether the results are due to the specific design of Automunge’s encodings or simply the act of preprocessing itself. For the paper to support its broader claim of improved preparation for deep learning, it must show that its methods outperform or complement established baselines. The authors should re-run the Higgs experiments including at least quantile transform and robust scaling, and report their AUC performance under the same train/test splits and model setup.

- No evaluation on heterogeneous or mixed-type datasets: All experiments are conducted on the Higgs dataset, which is nearly homogeneous in feature type (numeric) and very large in scale. This limits the relevance of the findings to real-world tabular problems, which often include mixed numeric-categorical features, missing values, and varied distributions. The paper claims applicability to 'numeric sets of unknown interpretation' and implies broad utility, but offers no evidence on more diverse benchmarks. To substantiate this, the authors should include results on at least one additional dataset—such as Adult (UCI), Telco Churn, or a healthcare dataset like MIMIC—where feature heterogeneity is present. This would better support the generalization claims and demonstrate the framework’s flexibility.

- No implementation guidance for encoding selection: While the paper describes many encoding options, it provides no practical guidance on how to choose among them for a given feature or dataset. The experiments apply uniform transformations across all features, which is unrealistic in practice. Readers will want to know how to decide between retain normalization, z-score, binning, or noise injection based on data properties. The authors should add a decision heuristic or flowchart in Section 10—e.g., based on skewness, zero prevalence, or data type—that maps observable feature characteristics to recommended encodings. This would transform the work from a catalog into a usable methodology.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include comparisons with standard preprocessing baselines (e.g., quantile transform, robust scaling) in the Higgs experiments.

- Conduct a proper ablation of data augmentation: separate the effects of sample duplication and noise injection.

- Reframe the novelty claim for noise injection to reflect its basis in existing regularization and privacy methods, and clarify the contribution as integration into a preprocessing pipeline.

- Provide a public code repository or detailed configuration scripts to reproduce all experiments and transformations.

- Evaluate the proposed methods on at least one additional diverse tabular dataset with mixed feature types.

comments.title: Incorrect MAD normalization formula
comments.quote: |  MAD | ‘MAD3’ | (xi-max)/(MAD) | scaled by median absolute deviation  |
comments.explanation: The formula (xi - max)/MAD incorrectly shifts by the maximum rather than the median, undermining the robustness intent of MAD-based scaling. The standard robust normalization is (xi - median)/MAD, which centers the data at the median. Using max introduces a large, asymmetric bias—especially in skewed data—and defeats the purpose of robustness. For example, in a set with an outlier, this would shift all values far from their central tendency. The numerator should use the median, not the max.

comments.title: Inconsistent lognorm transformation formula
comments.quote: |  lognorm | ‘lgnm’ | ln(xi) → (xi-μ)/σ | log-normal scaled to Gaussian  |
comments.explanation: The notation suggests the logarithm is applied but then ignored in the z-score, which uses the original xi instead of the logged values. This is incoherent: the z-score must be applied to ln(xi), not xi. The correct form is (ln(xi) - μ_y)/σ_y, where μ_y and σ_y are the mean and std of the logged values. As written, the transformation fails to normalize the distribution and misrepresents the method.

comments.title: Overstated zero-point retention in retain normalization
comments.quote: that bases the formula applied to scale data on the range of values found within the train set, with the result of scaling the data within a similar range as some of those demonstrated above while also retaining the zero point and thus the  $+ / -$  sign of all received data.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'retn' preserves the zero point and sign of all data is false for out-of-range values. When min > 0, a test value of 0 maps to a negative number; when max < 0, it maps to positive. Thus, the sign of zero is not preserved outside the training range. The method only preserves signs for values within or extrapolating from the training interval. This limitation should be explicitly acknowledged.

comments.title: Ambiguous handling of zero in mixed-sign retain normalization
comments.quote:
|  Min | Max | Formula | Returned Min | Returned Max  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  ≤0 | ≥0 | xi/(max-min) | min/(max-min) | max/(max-min)  |
comments.explanation: In the mixed-sign case, the transformation xi/(max - min) produces asymmetric scaling that depends on the relative magnitudes of |min| and |max|. For example, if min = -1 and max = 9, negative values are compressed more than positive ones. This asymmetry is not discussed and may introduce unintended bias. The paper should clarify that scaling is not symmetric and may affect model learning differently based on data range.

comments.title: No handling of constant or near-constant features
comments.quote: A common practice for preprocessing numeric feature sets for the application of neural networks is to apply a normalization operation in which received values are centered and scaled based on properties of the data.
comments.explanation: The paper does not address zero-variance or near-constant features, which would cause division by zero in all normalization methods (z-score, min-max, MAD, retn, etc.). Such features are common in real data (e.g., flags, identifiers). The paper should specify how Automunge handles them—e.g., by skipping normalization, returning zero, or adding epsilon—to ensure robustness in automated pipelines.

comments.title: Noise scaling in DPmm does not preserve range
comments.quote: noise source as implemented is Gaussian, the application is capped from extreme outliers at half of range (e.g.  $+/- 0.5$ ) and based on whether an input entry is above or below the midpoint, positive or negative noise respectively is scaled to ensure maintained original range in returned data based on values of input entry.
comments.explanation: The description implies that directional noise scaling preserves the original range, but without clamping or adaptive magnitude control, out-of-bounds values are inevitable. For example, adding +0.3 to a value at 0.9 in a [0,1] range yields 1.2. The mechanism as described does not guarantee range preservation. The text should clarify that outputs are clamped to the original bounds, or specify how noise magnitude is scaled relative to boundary proximity.

comments.title: Bernoulli flip allows self-replacement incorrectly
comments.quote: The application of noise to categoric encodings [Table 6, Fig 2] is a little simpler, where a given ratio of entries in a categoric feature set are flipped to one of the other activations, between which have a uniform probability of replacement (including possibility of original entry retention).
comments.explanation: Including the current activation in the 'flip' replacement undermines the intended perturbation. If a value is selected for noise with probability flip_prob, but then has a chance to remain unchanged, the effective perturbation rate is reduced. A true flip should transition to a different state. The mechanism should either exclude the current activation or be renamed to 'random replacement' to reflect its behavior.

comments.title: Capping noise at half-range misaligned with [0,1] bounds
comments.quote: , the application is capped from extreme outliers at half of range (e.g.  $+/- 0.5$ ) and
comments.explanation: The example of ±0.5 noise assumes a symmetric range like [-0.5, 0.5], but DPmm applies after min-max normalization to [0,1]. Adding +0.5 to a value near 1 exceeds the upper bound. The example is misleading and suggests an incorrect method. The text should remove the ±0.5 example and clarify that noise is constrained via clamping to [0,1] or asymmetric bounds based on input location.

comments.title: No handling of zero-range features in noise injection
comments.quote: noise source as implemented is Gaussian, the application is capped from extreme outliers at half of range (e.g.  $+/- 0.5$ ) and based on whether an input entry is above or below the midpoint, positive or negative noise respectively is scaled to ensure maintained original range in returned data based on values of input entry.
comments.explanation: The method assumes a non-degenerate range (max > min) to compute midpoint and scale noise. If a feature is constant (min = max), min-max normalization fails or maps all values to 0, and the midpoint-based noise rule breaks down. The paper does not specify how such cases are handled. Noise injection should be disabled or adapted for constant features to avoid undefined behavior.

comments.title: Inconsistent transform name 'por2' in family tree
comments.quote:
|   |  auntsuncles | [‘retn’, ‘1010’, ‘ordl’] | [‘retn’, ‘1010’, ‘ordl’, ‘pwr2’] | [‘retn’, ‘ordl’, ‘por2’] | [‘mnmx’]  |
|   |  cousins | [‘NAne’] | [‘NAne’] | [‘NAne’] | [‘NAne’]  |
comments.explanation: The transform 'por2' appears in the auntsuncles entry but is not defined anywhere, while 'pwr2' is explicitly defined in Table 7. Given the context and the use of 'pwr2' in a similar position, 'por2' is likely a typographical error. The downstream 'returned columns' also list 'column_por2', propagating the inconsistency. This should be corrected to 'pwr2' for clarity and consistency.

comments.title: Undefined 'NAne' transformation in cousins row
comments.quote:
|   |  cousins | [‘NAne’] | [‘NAne’] | [‘NAne’] | [‘NAne’]  |
|  downstream | children | [] | [] | [] | []  |
comments.explanation: The transformation 'NAne' is used in the cousins row but is not defined in the paper. While it may relate to missing data encoding, its purpose and behavior are unclear. The absence of a definition prevents understanding of its role in the transformation pipeline. The paper should define 'NAne' in Table 7 or remove it if redundant.

comments.title: Ambiguous 'returned columns' with mixed syntax
comments.quote:
|  returned columns | column_ord3_mnmx | column_ord3_mnmx | column_ord3_mnmx | (support entries for rtgr)  |   |
|   |   |  column_retn | column_retn |   | column_retn  |
|   | column_1010 (set) | column_1010 (set) | column_ordl |   |   |
comments.explanation: The 'returned columns' block mixes actual column names with explanatory comments like '(support entries for rtgr)' and uses ambiguous notation like 'column_1010 (set)'. This breaks the tabular format and makes it impossible to determine the actual output schema. For an automated pipeline, the outputs must be unambiguously specified as a list of column names.

comments.title: No handling of out-of-vocabulary integers in ordinal paths
comments.quote:
|
|  ‘dxdt’ | Sequential | Cumulative sequential  |
|  ‘ord3_mnmx’ | Frequency sorted ordinal followed by min-max | Scaled metric for ranking entry frequency  |
|  r
comments.explanation: The 'ordl' and 'ord3_mnmx' transforms assume a fixed ordinal mapping, but the paper does not specify how unseen integer values in test data are handled. For example, if training has {1,2,3} and test has 4, the encoding is undefined. Unlike continuous transforms, ordinal mappings cannot extrapolate without explicit rules. The paper should define a mechanism—such as clamping or OOV tokens—for such cases.

comments.title: Contradiction in noise injection implementation
comments.quote: Both of the noise injections applied Gaussian noise with standard deviation of 0.03 and with scaling to maintain the fixed range of the received data.
comments.explanation: The claim that Gaussian noise with σ=0.03 is scaled to 'maintain a fixed range' is contradictory: Gaussian noise is unbounded, and scaling alone cannot enforce range without clipping or truncation. If clipping is used, the distribution is no longer Gaussian. The paper must clarify whether clipping is applied or remove the claim of range preservation to avoid misrepresentation.

comments.title: Inconsistent labeling in Table 9
comments.quote: Retain w/ Augment partial
comments.explanation: Table 9 uses 'Retain w/ Augment partial', but the text only describes 'Retain w/ Augment' without 'partial'. This creates ambiguity: does 'partial' refer to partial noise or partial duplication? The rest of the paper uses the unqualified term. To maintain consistency, the label should be corrected to match the text or the distinction should be explained.

comments.title: Misleading open source claim for Automunge
comments.quote: This paper will offer arguments for potential benefits of extended encodings of numeric streams in deep learning by way of a survey of options for numeric transformations as available in the Automunge open source python library platform for tabular data pipelines
comments.explanation: The term 'open source' is used without providing a repository link, license, or version. Without public access to the code, the claim is unsubstantiated and misleads readers about reproducibility. The term should be removed unless a public, licensable repository is provided.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to prior work
comments.quote: It was the focus of a recent paper by this author *(Author, 2020)* to explore methods of preparing categoric sets for machine learning as are available in the Automunge open source python library platform for tabular data pipelines.
comments.explanation: The citation *(Author, 2020)* is not resolved in the references. Given that the author has related works from 2021 and 2022, this is likely a placeholder. The citation should be replaced with the correct reference or removed if no formal publication exists.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents the Automunge library for numeric feature encoding in tabular data, arguing that extended encodings beyond simple scaling can benefit deep learning performance. The paper surveys various encoding options including normalizations, binning, noise injection, sequential transforms, and integer encodings. The authors claim to demonstrate a novel data augmentation approach using noise injection that benefits models trained on underserved data. However, the experimental evaluation appears limited and insufficient to support the paper's claims.

The paper provides a comprehensive survey of numeric encoding techniques available in the Automunge library, with particular emphasis on a novel 'retain' normalization that preserves zero points and sign. The library appears to offer a flexible framework for feature engineering with an interesting family tree structure for composing transformations. However, the experimental validation is severely limited, using only one dataset with minimal statistical rigor, making it difficult to assess the practical significance of the claimed benefits.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient Experimental Evaluation: The paper's experimental evaluation is limited to a single dataset (Higgs) with only one neural network architecture. This is inadequate for demonstrating the broader claims about the benefits of various encoding techniques. The results show minimal differences between most methods (differences of 0.0001-0.001 in AUC), which may be within statistical variation. The claimed 'material benefit' of data augmentation (0.0106 AUC improvement at 0.25% data) needs validation across multiple datasets and architectures to be convincing. For a methodology paper at a top venue, we would expect comprehensive benchmarking across diverse datasets and model types to establish generalizability.

- Lack of Statistical Significance Testing: Table 8 reports performance metrics without any statistical significance testing. The paper mentions results are 'averages of repetition counts detailed in Appendix G' but provides no confidence intervals, p-values, or statistical tests to determine whether observed differences are meaningful. Given the small magnitude of most reported improvements (e.g., 0.0001-0.0003 AUC), statistical validation is essential. Without proper statistical analysis, readers cannot determine whether these improvements would replicate or are merely random variation. This is particularly important for the claimed novel finding about data augmentation benefits.

- Unclear Data Leakage Prevention: The paper states that transformations are 'directed for application to a distinct feature set' and mentions using train set properties for test set transformation, but provides insufficient detail about preventing data leakage. For binning operations that use quantiles ('bnep' - equal population bins), it's unclear how bin boundaries are determined for test data to avoid leakage. Similarly, for noise injection used for data augmentation, the paper doesn't clarify whether noise parameters are learned from training data only. Proper leakage prevention is critical for valid evaluation, especially with complex transformation pipelines that could inadvertently introduce test set information into training.

- Limited Baseline Comparisons: The experiments only compare against raw data and basic normalizations (z-score, min-max). Missing are comparisons with other established feature engineering approaches for tabular data, such as feature selection methods, interaction terms, polynomial features, or encodings from other libraries. The linear model results in Table 9 suggest bins help linear models, but there's no comparison with feature selection or regularization techniques that might achieve similar benefits more efficiently. Without broader baselines, it's difficult to assess whether Automunge's approach offers meaningful advantages over existing methods.

- Computational Overhead Unaddressed: The paper acknowledges memory overheads from redundant features but provides no analysis of computational costs. For methods like family tree transformations that can multiply feature columns, and for data augmentation that doubles dataset size, readers need quantitative analysis of training time and memory usage. The experiments mention 'small increase of training time' for bins but no numbers. For practical adoption, especially with large datasets, understanding the trade-offs between performance gains and computational costs is essential. This omission limits the paper's utility for practitioners.

- Questionable Novelty Claims: The paper claims 'a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning', but noise injection for tabular data has been explored in prior work (e.g., for differential privacy as cited). What's novel appears to be the specific implementation within Automunge's framework, not the fundamental approach. Similarly, the 'retain' normalization preserves zero points, which is a reasonable design choice but may not constitute significant novelty. The paper would benefit from more precise positioning of its contributions relative to existing work on tabular data augmentation and feature engineering.

- Missing worked example of family tree transformations: The paper introduces the family tree primitive system but never walks through a complete end-to-end example with actual data. Readers need to see how these primitives compose in practice with concrete numbers. For instance, take the 'ntgr' integer encoding example from Figure 4 and show the step-by-step transformation of a sample integer column (e.g., [1, 2, 5, 10, 100]) through each stage: how the ord3 encoding maps values, how the retn normalization scales them, how the 1010 binary encoding represents them, and what the final augmented feature set looks like. This would clarify the system's mechanics and demonstrate the claimed benefit of redundant encoding.

- No demonstration of when transformations fail: The paper only shows cases where transformations work but never demonstrates their limitations or failure modes. For a methodology paper, readers need to understand when NOT to use these techniques. For example, show how z-score normalization performs poorly with heavy-tailed distributions, or how binning destroys information for certain patterns. A concrete counterexample would be: generate synthetic data where the relationship between features and target is non-linear and gets lost after min-max scaling, demonstrating the trade-offs. This would provide crucial guidance for practitioners and strengthen the paper's credibility.

- Missing implementation guidance for noise injection parameters: The paper claims noise injection provides 'material benefit' but offers no guidance on selecting parameters like noise standard deviation or flip probability. The experiments use σ=0.03 without justification. Readers need a systematic analysis of how these parameters affect performance across different data scales and noise sensitivities. For instance, show performance curves for various σ values on the Higgs dataset, or provide a rule-of-thumb for setting σ based on feature variance. Without this guidance, practitioners cannot effectively apply the claimed novel augmentation technique.

- No verification of special case recovery: The paper positions its methods as generalizations of standard techniques but never verifies that established results are recovered as special cases. For example, the 'retn' normalization should reduce to min-max scaling when all values are positive, and z-score should be recovered from 'nmbr' with appropriate parameter choices. The paper should demonstrate these equivalences mathematically and empirically on a simple dataset. This verification would establish the framework's consistency with known methods and build confidence in its extensions.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Expand experimental evaluation to multiple diverse datasets (at least 5-7 covering different domains and data characteristics)

- Add statistical significance testing with confidence intervals or p-values for all reported performance differences

- Provide detailed description and validation of data leakage prevention mechanisms for all transformation types

- Include computational cost analysis (training time, memory usage) for different encoding approaches

- Add comparisons with established baseline methods beyond basic normalizations

comments.title: Retain normalization formula error
comments.quote:
Table 2: Retain Normalization ('retn')

|  Min | Max | Formula | Returned Min | Returned Max  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  ≤0 | ≥0 | xi / (max-min) | min / (max-min) | max / (max-min)  |
comments.explanation: The formula xi/(max-min) for the case Min ≤ 0 and Max ≥ 0 does not achieve the stated goal of preserving zero while scaling to a standard range. For xi = 0, the formula returns 0, preserving zero. However, the scaling is inconsistent with the paper's claim of 'retaining the zero point and thus the +/- sign of all received data' in a meaningful way. The formula maps max to max/(max-min), which equals 1 only when min = 0. For example, with min = -2 and max = 3, max maps to 3/5 = 0.6. The correct formula to preserve zero while scaling to [-1, 1] should be xi / max(|min|, |max|).

comments.title: Lognorm formula missing log operation
comments.quote: |  lognorm | ‘lgnm’ | ln(xi) then (xi-μ)/σ | log-normal scaled to Gaussian  |
comments.explanation: The formula description for lognorm is mathematically incorrect. It states 'ln(xi) then (xi-μ)/σ' but uses xi in both operations. After taking ln(xi), the subsequent normalization should operate on the log-transformed values. The correct procedure is: first compute yi = ln(xi), then apply (yi-μ_y)/σ_y where μ_y and σ_y are the mean and standard deviation of the log-transformed values.

comments.title: Missing mathematical definition of noise injection
comments.quote: The options available for noise injection are generally derived by way of aggregations of transformation category entries to family tree primitives, as noise injection may be applied downstream to a normalization or categoric encoding.
comments.explanation: The section describes the implementation architecture but lacks a formal mathematical definition of the noise injection operations. For reproducibility, the paper should define the noise injection functions mathematically. For example, for DPmm applied to normalized data x ∈ [0,1], the function should be specified as: f(x) = x + s·clip(N(0,σ²), -0.5, 0.5) where s is the scaling factor.

comments.title: Unclear noise scaling mechanism
comments.quote: noise source as implemented is Gaussian, the application is capped from extreme outliers at half of range (e.g.  $+/- 0.5$ ) and based on whether an input entry is above or below the midpoint, positive or negative noise respectively is scaled to ensure maintained original range in returned data based on values of input entry.
comments.explanation: The scaling mechanism is mathematically underspecified. For input x ∈ [0,1] and Gaussian noise N(0,σ²), the text states that noise is 'scaled to ensure maintained original range' but doesn't define the scaling function. If x < 0.5, negative noise is applied, but the scaling factor that guarantees x + scaled_noise ∈ [0,1] is unclear. The scaling could be min(x, 1-x) or another function, but without explicit definition, the range preservation property cannot be verified.

comments.title: Missing statistical significance testing
comments.quote: The AUC metrics shown are averages of repetition counts detailed in Appendix G.
comments.explanation: The paper reports AUC metrics as averages but provides no statistical significance testing. With small differences between methods (e.g., 0.0002-0.0017), it's unclear whether observed differences are statistically meaningful or within experimental variation. The paper should include confidence intervals or p-values to determine if differences between preprocessing methods are significant, especially when claiming benefits for data augmentation.

comments.title: Overstated novelty claim for noise injection
comments.quote: Experiments demonstrate the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning, as may materially benefit model performance in applications with underserved training data.
comments.explanation: The claim of a 'novel generalized solution' for noise injection in tabular learning appears overstated. Noise injection for tabular data augmentation has been explored in prior work, including applications in differential privacy and regularization. The paper should either qualify this claim to specify what aspects are novel (e.g., the specific implementation within Automunge's framework) or provide citations demonstrating how this approach differs fundamentally from existing noise injection methods.

comments.title: Returned range inconsistency in Table 2
comments.quote: |  ≤0 | ≥0 | xi / (max-min) | min / (max-min) | max / (max-min)  |
comments.explanation: The returned min and max values for the first row of Table 2 are inconsistent with the paper's claim of scaling data 'within a similar range as some of those demonstrated above.' For the formula xi/(max-min), the output range depends on the specific values of min and max. For example, if min = -2 and max = 3, then max-min = 5, so min/(max-min) = -0.4 and max/(max-min) = 0.6. This range [-0.4, 0.6] is not comparable to the standard ranges achieved by other normalizations in Table 1.

comments.title: Mean normalization formula inconsistency
comments.quote: |  mean | ‘mean’ | (xi-mean)/(max-min) | scaled and centered to mean  |
comments.explanation: The mean normalization formula (xi-mean)/(max-min) does not actually center the data to have mean 0. When you subtract the mean and divide by (max-min), the resulting transformed data will not have mean 0 unless the original data was uniformly distributed. The description 'scaled and centered to mean' is misleading. It should be described as 'shifted by mean and scaled by range' because the formula does not guarantee zero mean for the transformed data.

comments.title: MAD normalization missing absolute value
comments.quote: |  MAD | ‘MAD3’ | (xi-median)/(MAD) | scaled by median absolute deviation  |
comments.explanation: The MAD normalization formula (xi-median)/(MAD) is incomplete. MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) is typically defined as the median of absolute deviations from the median: MAD = median(|xi - median|). The paper's description doesn't specify that MAD involves absolute values. Without the absolute value in the MAD calculation, the denominator could be zero or negative, which would break the normalization.

comments.title: Ambiguous categoric noise replacement probability
comments.quote: For the 'DP10' root category example shown in Fig 2, this is achieved by first applying an ordinal encoding by ord3 transformation function associated with a 'DPo6' transformation category entry to the parents upstream primitive of the 'DP10' root category, then a noise injection by the DPod transformation function associated with a 'DPo3' transformation category to the downstream children primitive associated with the 'DPo6' root category (applied since parents is a primitive with offspring), followed by a binary encoding by the 1010 transformation function associated with the '1010' transformation category entry to the downstream coworkers primitive associated with the 'DPo3' root category (applied since children is a primitive with offspring).
comments.explanation: The probability mechanism is mathematically inconsistent as stated. If flip_prob determines the ratio of entries selected for potential flipping, and each selected entry has uniform probability among k activations including the original, then the actual change probability is flip_prob × (k-1)/k. The text should clarify whether flip_prob refers to the probability of attempting a flip or the probability of actual change.

The header row defines 3 columns ('Transform', 'Type', 'Useful For') but the 'root category' row contains 6 entries without proper markdown table syntax. This creates a structural inconsistency where the table layout doesn't match the intended content organization.

comments.title: Inconsistent delta notation in Table 8
comments.quote:
Table 8: Higgs Data Normalization Scenarios (AUC metric // compared to raw data)

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a survey of numeric feature transformation strategies implemented in the Automunge library, focusing on normalization, noise injection, and binning techniques for tabular data. While the library offers a practical framework for feature engineering, the paper lacks a rigorous empirical foundation to support its claims regarding the efficacy of these transformations.

The paper provides a useful overview of a library-specific preprocessing pipeline. However, it fails to provide the depth of evidence required for a conference publication, specifically regarding the performance impact of its proposed encoding methods. The experimental results are limited to a single dataset and do not adequately justify the complexity introduced by the proposed 'family tree' transformations.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient Experimental Scope and Benchmarking: The experimental evaluation in Section 8 relies exclusively on the Higgs dataset. This is insufficient to demonstrate the general efficacy of the proposed numeric encoding strategies, as the paper claims these methods are applicable to diverse tabular data types. To be convincing, the authors should include a broader suite of benchmarks, such as those commonly used in tabular deep learning research (e.g., datasets from the UCI repository or the recent tabular DL survey benchmarks). Without this, it is impossible to determine if the reported results generalize beyond high-energy physics data.

- Conflation of Data Augmentation with Noise Injection: In Section 6, the authors claim that noise injection acts as a generalized solution for data augmentation. However, the results in Table 8 show that noise injection often degrades model performance compared to the raw data baseline. The paper does not provide a theoretical or empirical analysis of the trade-off between the regularization benefits of noise and the signal-to-noise ratio degradation. The authors should provide a more nuanced discussion or a sensitivity analysis showing under what specific data regimes (e.g., sample size, feature sparsity) noise injection provides a net benefit.

- Lack of Ablation Studies: The paper introduces several complex 'family tree' transformation structures, yet it does not isolate the impact of individual components. For instance, it is unclear whether the performance gains (or lack thereof) are due to the specific normalization used, the binning strategy, or the interaction between them. An ablation study is necessary to justify the inclusion of these specific transformation primitives. Without isolating these effects, the reader cannot discern which parts of the Automunge pipeline are actually contributing to model performance.

- Potential for Data Leakage: The methodology assumes that transformations are based on properties derived from the 'train' set and applied to the 'test' set. However, the paper does not address the risk of data leakage when these transformations (like binning or z-score normalization) are applied to non-stationary data distributions. In real-world applications where the test distribution shifts, these transformations could introduce significant bias. The authors should explicitly discuss how their pipeline handles test-time distribution shifts or demonstrate that their implementation is robust to such changes.

- Lack of concrete implementation guidance for custom transformation integration: The paper describes a 'transformdict' structure for custom user configurations but fails to provide a minimal, working code example of how a user would define and register a custom transformation function. As this is a framework paper, readers need to see the syntax required to integrate a novel transformation into the 'family tree' logic. I suggest adding a code snippet illustrating a simple custom transformation, such as a square-root or clipping function, and showing how it is registered within the 'transformdict' to demonstrate the library's extensibility.

- Missing computational complexity analysis for high-cardinality feature sets: The paper claims the Automunge pipeline is suitable for diverse tabular datasets, yet it ignores the computational overhead of generating 'family tree' derivations for high-dimensional or high-cardinality inputs. As the number of columns and transformation branches grows, the memory footprint and processing time could become prohibitive. To address this, the authors should provide a brief complexity analysis or a scalability plot showing how the transformation time and memory usage scale with the number of features and rows on a standard dataset, such as the Adult or Covertype datasets.

- Absence of a worked example for sequential data transformation: Section 6 introduces 'dxdt' transforms for sequential data but provides no concrete example of their application or effect on a time-series dataset. Without a clear illustration, it is difficult to verify if these transformations correctly handle the temporal dependencies or if they introduce artifacts. I recommend adding a small, synthetic time-series example where the 'dxdt' transformation is applied, explicitly showing the input series and the resulting derived feature columns to demonstrate that the velocity proxies are computed as intended.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper currently reads more like a library documentation manual than a research contribution. It requires a more rigorous experimental section with multiple datasets and a clearer theoretical justification for why these specific numeric encodings outperform standard industry practices.

Revision targets:

- Expand the experimental section to include at least three additional, diverse tabular datasets.

- Perform ablation studies to isolate the impact of binning versus normalization versus noise injection.

- Add a section discussing the robustness of the transformations against non-stationary data and potential leakage.

- Provide a direct comparison against state-of-the-art preprocessing methods (e.g., quantile transforms, power transforms) rather than just comparing to raw data.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper surveys numeric preprocessing transformations available in the Automunge library and evaluates their impact on tabular learning using the Higgs dataset. While the practical focus on feature engineering for tabular data is relevant, the manuscript currently reads more like a software documentation overview than a rigorous research contribution. The experimental design lacks statistical validation, relies on an undertrained neural network, and omits standard baselines. The claims of novelty regarding noise injection and the theoretical justification for specific transformations require substantial strengthening.

The paper addresses a practical gap in tabular data preprocessing by systematically cataloging numeric transformations and testing them on a large-scale physics dataset. The modular architecture for chaining operations is an interesting engineering choice, and the empirical comparison across multiple scaling and binning strategies provides useful practical insights. However, the manuscript overstates its novelty, lacks statistical rigor in its evaluation, and does not sufficiently connect its library design to established learning theory or modern tabular baselines.

Overview issues:

- Overstated novelty claim for tabular noise injection: The abstract and Section 5 assert a novel generalized solution for data augmentation via noise injection in tabular learning. This claim overlooks a substantial body of existing work on adding Gaussian noise, Laplace noise, or synthetic samples to tabular datasets, as well as modern generative augmentation techniques like CTGAN and diffusion models. The paper does not differentiate its specific implementation from these established methods, nor does it provide a theoretical or empirical analysis of why the proposed Gaussian-with-Bernoulli-ratio approach outperforms standard noise baselines. The authors should explicitly contrast their method with prior tabular augmentation literature and clarify which specific mechanism or parameterization constitutes the actual novelty.

- Marginal performance gains lack statistical validation: Table 8 reports performance improvements from noise augmentation on underserved data, but the deltas are small (0.0017 to 0.0106) and presented without confidence intervals, standard deviations, or p-values. Claiming a material benefit based on such narrow margins is statistically unsound, especially given the high variance typical in tabular deep learning experiments. The repetition counts mentioned in Appendix G are not summarized in the main text, making it impossible to assess whether these gains are reproducible or noise. The authors must report mean and standard deviation across all repetitions, run appropriate statistical tests against the baseline, and temper their conclusions to reflect the actual magnitude of improvement.

- Inadequate training protocol and missing baselines: Section 8 explicitly states that no significant attempt was made to train models to maximum performance, limiting the neural network to 3-42 epochs. This protocol makes it difficult to disentangle the effects of preprocessing from optimization dynamics, as poorly trained models are highly sensitive to initialization and learning rate schedules. Furthermore, the evaluation omits strong gradient-boosted baselines like XGBoost or LightGBM, and modern tabular architectures such as TabNet or FT-Transformer. Without these comparisons, the paper cannot demonstrate whether its preprocessing pipeline offers a genuine advantage over state-of-the-art tabular methods. The revision should include properly converged training runs, learning curves to show convergence behavior, and comparisons against established tabular benchmarks.

- Ad-hoc normalization design lacks theoretical grounding: Section 2 introduces the 'retn' normalization and Table 2 provides formulas that appear inconsistent with standard scaling practices. For mixed-sign data, the formula `xi/(max-min)` does not clearly preserve the original zero point or sign structure as claimed, and the table's returned min/max values do not align with the proposed scaling. The paper argues that preserving the mathematical properties of zero aids backpropagation, but offers no gradient analysis, convergence proof, or ablation study to support this hypothesis. The authors should either provide a formal derivation showing how this transformation affects the loss landscape or replace the theoretical justification with empirical evidence demonstrating its specific utility.

- Library documentation framing overshadows research contribution: Sections 3 through 7 dedicate significant space to describing Automunge's internal architecture, including family tree primitives, processdict structures, and NArowtype classifications. This reads as a software manual rather than a research analysis, and it fails to connect these engineering choices to learnable representations or generalizable machine learning principles. The paper would benefit from abstracting the library-specific implementation details and focusing instead on the underlying transformation categories and their impact on model capacity or generalization. Reframing the manuscript to prioritize learning dynamics over software architecture would better align it with conference expectations.

- Speculative link between early SGD dynamics and feature engineering: The discussion in Section 9 cites Nakkiran et al. (2019) to argue that early-stage linear learning justifies manual feature engineering. While this connection is conceptually interesting, the paper provides no empirical analysis of training dynamics to support it. There are no learning curves, no analysis of early versus late epoch performance relative to preprocessing choices, and no measurement of how quickly different encodings converge. The authors should either add experiments tracking validation metrics across epochs to demonstrate the claimed early-stage benefit or remove the speculative reference to avoid overreaching beyond the presented evidence.

- Missing evaluation of sequential transforms on time-series data: Section 6 introduces sequential transforms like dxdt to extract derivatives and smoothing from ordered data. The experiments in Section 8, however, rely exclusively on the Higgs dataset, which lacks any temporal structure. This leaves the core utility of the sequential module entirely unverified. Readers will naturally ask how these transforms perform when time deltas or cyclic properties actually drive the target variable. The authors should run a dedicated experiment on a standard time-series benchmark or a synthetic autoregressive process. Reporting validation metrics for the dxdt family on ordered data would validate the section's claims and demonstrate practical utility beyond the static tabular results.

- No guidance for selecting noise injection hyperparameters: Despite claiming that noise injection materially benefits underserved training data, the experimental protocol fixes the Gaussian noise standard deviation at 0.03 without justification. This fixed value offers no insight into how practitioners should tune the injection strength relative to feature variance or dataset scale. A natural question is whether this noise level generalizes across domains with different numerical ranges. The authors need to include a sensitivity analysis showing performance across a range of noise standard deviations. Providing a heuristic or automated rule for setting these parameters would transform the method from a fixed recipe into a genuinely generalized solution.

- Untested integer encoding strategy on discrete features: Readers will find the integer encoding strategy in Section 7 completely unverified because the experiments run exclusively on the Higgs dataset, which consists primarily of continuous float features. This leaves the proposed redundant encodings across continuous, ordinal, binary, and sequential representations entirely untested on their intended use case. The authors should construct a synthetic dataset with known integer structures or select a benchmark with prominent discrete numeric columns. Comparing the redundant encoding against single-encoding baselines on such data would validate this specific contribution and clarify when the redundancy actually improves generalization.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Replace the novelty claim for noise injection with a clear comparison to existing tabular augmentation methods and specify the exact contribution.

- Report mean and standard deviation for all experimental results, include statistical significance testing, and adjust conclusions to match the observed effect sizes.

- Re-run experiments with properly converged training protocols, add learning curves, and include baselines from gradient boosting and modern tabular architectures.

- Provide formal justification or empirical ablation for the 'retn' normalization, clarifying its mathematical properties and impact on optimization.

- Reframe the manuscript to focus on learning dynamics and generalization rather than library implementation details, removing excessive software documentation.

comments.title: Conflation of noise injection and data augmentation in results
comments.quote: As expected the noise injection had a dampening effect on the model accuracy, although the partial injection had only a small penalty in comparison to the retain normalization without injection. Data augmentation improved the results, sufficiently to draw the conclusion of material benefit for scenarios with underserved training data. We believe the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning is a significant finding.
comments.explanation: The paragraph incorrectly attributes the performance improvement to "data augmentation by noise injection." Table 8 clearly shows that noise injection alone ("Retain w/ Noise full" and "Retain w/ Noise partial") reduces AUC compared to the "Retain" baseline. The improvement is only observed in the "Retain w/ Augment" scenario, which the text describes as "doubling the number of samples." The authors are conflating two distinct mechanisms. Rewrite the conclusion to clearly distinguish between the dampening effect of noise injection and the marginal benefit of sample duplication, or explicitly state how the augmentation scenario combines these techniques.

comments.title: Mathematical error in MAD normalization formula
comments.quote: |  MAD | ‘MAD3’ | (xi-max)/(MAD) | scaled by median absolute deviation  |
comments.explanation: The formula `(xi-max)/(MAD)` centers the data at the maximum value, which contradicts the standard definition and stated purpose of Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) scaling. Robust MAD normalization universally subtracts the median from each observation before dividing by the MAD to achieve a median-centered, scale-invariant transformation. Using `max` fundamentally alters the statistical property being preserved. Rewrite `(xi-max)/(MAD)` as `(xi-median)/(MAD)` because the former incorrectly centers the data at the maximum value rather than the median, contradicting the standard definition of robust MAD scaling.

comments.quote:
